subject: [PATCH 3/4] btrfs: handle ENOMEM from btrfs_insert_dir_item() without aborting

reviews.author: Boris Burkov
reviews.summary: Reviewer Boris Burkov questioned whether it's possible for delayed dir index pre-allocation to fail, but btrfs_orphan_add() to succeed due to its own memory allocations, and asked if the issue has been reproduced in practice.
reviews.raw_body:
I feel like the crux of this series to me is whether you have practical
conditions where the allocation of the delayed_node is failing, but the
allocations involved in btrfs_orphan_add() succeed. It allocates a
btrfs_path and has to walk the btree which might have to read the node
at every level which might need to allocate 16k extent buffers and
extent buffer objects and xarray storage for each one. For size
reference, on my build (maybe debug..?) a delayed_node is 552 bytes,
while a btrfs_path is 112 and an extent_buffer is 432. So they are
pretty similar in size (not to mention the 16k of node file backed
memory we are sort of likely to have to allocate if we are under
reclaim)

Were you able to reproduce this issue and help in practice or is this a
theoretical / structural improvement?

With that said, all the prealloc wiring looks good to me in general, and
it seems to be a pretty clean win for the "name exists" case in the next
patch.

Thanks,
Boris

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the patch's effectiveness in preventing filesystem aborts under heavy memory pressure, acknowledging it's difficult to reproduce but noting there are existing records of such incidents and proposing to use ALLOW_ERROR_INJECTION() calls to make testing easier.
reviews.raw_body:
I didn't really try to reproduce this in earnest. We only see it in our
fleet under heavy memory pressure, and even then at such low frequency,
I doubt our chances of hitting this on anything other than a huge set
of machines.

So, theoretical / structural, but we have record of filesystem aborts
where the stack indicates that this would have prevented it. Userland
would have gotten an -ENOMEM back but the fs wouldn't have aborted.

I see that there are some ALLOW_ERROR_INJECTION() calls in btrfs. We
could wire some of these functions up with that, which would make this
easier to test. I'll look into that in the meantime.

reviews.author: Boris Burkov
reviews.summary: Reviewer Boris Burkov expressed concern that handling ENOMEM in btrfs_add_link() may not be effective, as it could still lead to an abort when calling btrfs_orphan_add(), and requested concrete evidence of its benefits.
reviews.raw_body:
My concern is not that we don't hit ENOMEM in btrfs_add_link(), since
like you said we can observe that in abort logs. I am worried that even
if we try to handle it gracefully, we will just ENOMEM in
btrfs_orphan_add() and abort anyway. That is why I was wanting to see
some more concrete evidence this actually helps to make it worth the
complexity.

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged a potential issue where objects are allocated successfully but then fail to be added as orphans due to an error in btrfs_orphan_add(), and suggested preallocating the main allocation (btrfs_alloc_path?) in the transaction handle.
reviews.raw_body:
In the case where we handle this gracefully, we won't hit that because
it will have returned -ENOMEM before that point. But, you do have a
good point that we could allocate these objects successfully, and then
hit an error in btrfs_orphan_add() anyway.

One thought: It looks like the main allocation in that codepath is
btrfs_alloc_path()? We could consider preallocating that too -- maybe
stash it in a new pointer in btrfs_trans_handle?

Thanks! This is good food for thought.

reviews.author: Boris Burkov
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Boris Burkov noted that when handling ENOMEM from btrfs_insert_dir_item(), the patch calls btrfs_orphan_add() which itself may fail and return ENOMEM, potentially leading to an infinite loop of errors.

Reviewer Boris Burkov expressed confusion about the patch's handling of ENOMEM in btrfs_insert_dir_item(), specifically asking for clarification on the inline comments he made earlier.

Reviewer pointed out that the patch description and code changes do not match, as the patch description mentions delayed dir index pre-allocation but the code does not implement it.
reviews.raw_body:
And when we get ENOMEM, we call btrfs_orphan_add() isntead of
unilaterally aborting. I contend that this call is now likely to ENOMEM

---

I am still a bit confused. I made comments inline closer to the the code.
Sorry if I am being dense or missing the point!

---

Take a peek at https://lore.kernel.org/linux-btrfs/cover.1782249000.git.boris@bur.io/

which I need to RESEND :)

Thanks,
Boris

2026-07-17.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Btrfs file system to handle ENOMEM errors more gracefully when inserting directory items. Instead of aborting transactions under memory pressure, it converts newly-created inodes into orphans and returns -ENOMEM to userspace. This change affects create(), mkdir(), mknod(), symlink(), and link() operations. The approach taken involves modifying the btrfs_add_link() function to handle ENOMEM errors and adding orphan handling in btrfs_create_new_inode().

reviews.author: Qu Wenruo
reviews.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested using on-stack memory for btrfs_alloc_path() to avoid potential trans aborts due to ENOMEM, citing a similar practice in btrfs-progs and the relatively small size of the structure (112 bytes).
reviews.raw_body:
For btrfs_alloc_path(), you can just use on-stack memory for that.

That's the practice we utilize for btrfs-progs, and it's still 
acceptable for kernel, since that structure is only 112 bytes.

We should not switch all btrfs_path to on-stack ones, but for critical 
ones that memory allocation can lead to trans abort, I'd say it's 
definitely worth the extra on-stack memory usage.

Thanks,
Qu

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged a need to restructure the code in response to reviewer feedback, agreeing to make changes in v2.
sentiment_signals: acknowledged a need for further work
reviews.raw_body:
Interesting. Between that approach and Qu's suggestion we should be
able fix this up. I'll look at doing that for v2.

Thanks again for the review!

-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

2026-07-18.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Btrfs file system to handle ENOMEM errors more gracefully when inserting directory items. Instead of aborting transactions under memory pressure, it converts newly-created inodes into orphans and returns -ENOMEM to userspace. This change affects create(), mkdir(), mknod(), symlink(), and link() operations. The approach taken involves modifying the btrfs_add_link() function to handle ENOMEM errors and adding orphan handling in btrfs_create_new_inode().

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary: The author is addressing Boris Burkov's concern about the later failure mode, and plans to rebase this series on top of Boris' series once it is resent.
reviews.raw_body:
I think basing this series on top of yours would make the most sense.
That should take care of your main concern with the later failure mode
since your series should fix most of those cases. I'll plan to rebase
this on top of that series once you resend it.

Thanks!
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

2026-07-21.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Btrfs file system to handle ENOMEM errors more gracefully when inserting directory items. Instead of aborting transactions under memory pressure, it converts newly-created inodes into orphans and returns -ENOMEM to userspace. This change affects create(), mkdir(), mknod(), symlink(), and link() operations. The approach taken involves modifying the btrfs_add_link() function to handle ENOMEM errors and adding orphan handling in btrfs_create_new_inode().
